title_en: ‘I Suppose It’s Just the Flow of the Times' — Yoko Taro Urges Players to Buy Physical Games as PlayStation Plans to End Disc Distribution
summary_en: Yoko Taro took to X on July 19 to bemoan the dire future of physical media following Sony’s decision to end production of game discs starting January 2028.
body_en:
Yoko Taro is just as devastated about physical games going the way of the dodo as we are, apparently.
The Nier creative director and scenario writer took to X (formerly Twitter) on July 19 to bemoan the dire future of physical media following Sony’s decision to end production of game discs starting January 2028.
“Personally, I can't help but feel sad about physical media disappearing, but I suppose it's just the flow of the times,” he penned, as per a machine translation.
“Since controlling physical distribution channels is an inherent advantage — especially compared to platforms like Steam — I had actually envisioned a future where, even as digital downloads became the norm, high-priced limited editions would remain physical (much like the model for CD distribution). It’s a shame my prediction was wrong.”
個人的に物理メディアがなくなるのはさみしい限りだけど、時代の流れなんでしょうね。
物理流通を握ってる事自体は steam などに比べてアドバンテージと言えるから、ＤＬ化が推進されても高額な限定版だけ物理、みたいな（ＣＤ流通的な）未来が来ると思ってたけど。ハズレて残念。 https://t.co/25PmkG836m
Taro wrote this statement in a quote-retweet of his own post, where he excitedly showed off his physical copy of Hi-Fi Rush and urged players to get their hands on actual game discs while they still can.
“With the recent buzz about the PS5 discontinuing physical media production, we've received Hi-Fi RUSH from our last hope, Super Deluxe-san! Don't assume your parents and physical media will always be there, so if you're interested, be sure to check it out,” he said.
Taro isn’t alone in his thinking. Players around the world are speaking out against the death of physical game discs online, to the point where new game releases are being bogged down with comments protesting PlayStation’s industry-changing business decision.
Sony is even facing a few lawsuits over the ordeal, with a Dutch consumer interest group and Mexican lawmakers arguing the death of physical discs could spell out a “relative monopolistic practice” that throttles prices and puts independent game stores out of business.
(Photo by Christian Petersen/Getty Images)
Virginia (she/her) is IGN’s News Editor. With ten years of experience reporting on games and entertainment, she’s got a storied background in the fighting game community, influencer news, and viral online trends. Find her on Twitter at @TheeMissGlaze .
paragraphs_en: Yoko Taro is just as devastated about physical games going the way of the dodo as we are, apparently.

The Nier creative director and scenario writer took to X (formerly Twitter) on July 19 to bemoan the dire future of physical media following Sony’s decision to end production of game discs starting January 2028.
“Personally, I can't help but feel sad about physical media disappearing, but I suppose it's just the flow of the times,” he penned, as per a machine translation.

“Since controlling physical distribution channels is an inherent advantage — especially compared to platforms like Steam — I had actually envisioned a future where, even as digital downloads became the norm, high-priced limited editions would remain physical (much like the model for CD distribution). It’s a shame my prediction was wrong.”
個人的に物理メディアがなくなるのはさみしい限りだけど、時代の流れなんでしょうね。
物理流通を握ってる事自体は steam などに比べてアドバンテージと言えるから、ＤＬ化が推進されても高額な限定版だけ物理、みたいな（ＣＤ流通的な）未来が来ると思ってたけど。ハズレて残念。 https://t.co/25PmkG836m

Taro wrote this statement in a quote-retweet of his own post, where he excitedly showed off his physical copy of Hi-Fi Rush and urged players to get their hands on actual game discs while they still can.

“With the recent buzz about the PS5 discontinuing physical media production, we've received Hi-Fi RUSH from our last hope, Super Deluxe-san! Don't assume your parents and physical media will always be there, so if you're interested, be sure to check it out,” he said.

Taro isn’t alone in his thinking. Players around the world are speaking out against the death of physical game discs online, to the point where new game releases are being bogged down with comments protesting PlayStation’s industry-changing business decision.

Sony is even facing a few lawsuits over the ordeal, with a Dutch consumer interest group and Mexican lawmakers arguing the death of physical discs could spell out a “relative monopolistic practice” that throttles prices and puts independent game stores out of business.
